input: How do I calculate year-over-year sales growth in Power BI?
actual_output: To calculate year-over-year sales growth, create a DAX measure: YoY Growth = DIVIDE([Total Sales] - CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])), CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))). This requires a properly configured Date table marked as a date table.
expected_output: Create a DAX measure using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR: YoY Growth = DIVIDE([Total Sales] - CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])), CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))). Ensure you have a Date table marked as a date table in your model.

input: Write a DAX formula for a running total of sales by month.
actual_output: Use CALCULATE with DATESYTD for a year-to-date running total: Running Total = CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD('Date'[Date])). For a cumulative running total across all time, use: Running Total = CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL('Date'), 'Date'[Date] <= MAX('Date'[Date]))).
expected_output: Running Total = CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL('Date'), 'Date'[Date] <= MAX('Date'[Date]))). Alternatively, for YTD: CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD('Date'[Date])).

input: What chart type should I use to show sales trends over time?
actual_output: For showing sales trends over time, use a Line Chart. Place your date field on the X-axis and your sales measure on the Y-axis. If you want to compare multiple categories, use a multi-line chart by adding a category to the Legend field.
expected_output: Use a Line Chart with date on the X-axis and sales on the Y-axis. For comparing categories over time, add a Legend field to create multiple lines.

input: How do I add conditional formatting to a table in Power BI?
actual_output: Select the table visual, go to the Format pane, expand the Cell elements section, and enable Background color or Font color. Choose 'Rules' or 'Gradient' to define your conditions. You can also right-click a column header and select Conditional formatting for more options.
expected_output: In the Format pane under Cell elements, enable Background color or Font color. Use Rules or Gradient mode to set conditions. Alternatively, right-click a column header and choose Conditional formatting.

input: What is a star schema and why should I use it in Power BI?
actual_output: A star schema is a data modeling pattern with a central fact table connected to dimension tables. In Power BI, star schemas improve query performance because the engine optimizes for this pattern, make DAX calculations simpler and more predictable, and create a cleaner, more maintainable model.
expected_output: A star schema has a central fact table surrounded by dimension tables. Benefits in Power BI: better query performance, simpler DAX formulas, and easier model maintenance.

input: How do I create a many-to-many relationship in Power BI?
actual_output: In Power BI, create a many-to-many relationship by going to Model view, dragging from one table to another, and setting the cardinality to Many-to-Many. You can also use a bridge table: create an intermediate table with unique combinations of the keys, then create one-to-many relationships from each original table to the bridge table.
expected_output: Set cardinality to Many-to-Many in the relationship dialog in Model view. Alternatively, use a bridge table with one-to-many relationships to each original table for better performance.

input: How do I create a drill-through page in Power BI?
actual_output: Create a new page, add the fields you want as detail. Then drag a field to the Drill-through filters well on that page. Users can then right-click a data point on other pages and select 'Drill through' to navigate to your detail page, filtered by that data point.
expected_output: Create a new page, add detail visuals, and drag a field into the Drill-through filters well. Users right-click data points on other pages to drill through to the filtered detail page.

input: How do I use bookmarks to create a toggle between two visuals?
actual_output: Create two overlapping visuals. Show the first, hide the second, and create Bookmark A. Then hide the first, show the second, and create Bookmark B. Add two buttons and assign each button's Action to one of the bookmarks. In both bookmarks, set 'Data' to unchecked so only the display state toggles.
expected_output: Overlap two visuals. Create bookmarks for each visibility state. Add buttons with Action set to each bookmark. Uncheck 'Data' in bookmark settings so filters are not affected.

input: Why is my Power BI report running slowly?
actual_output: Common causes of slow Power BI reports: too many visuals on a single page (keep under 8), complex DAX measures using CALCULATE with large tables, importing data when DirectQuery would reduce model size, missing relationships causing cross-joins, and not using aggregations. Use Performance Analyzer in the View tab to identify slow visuals.
expected_output: Check for: too many visuals per page, complex DAX measures, unnecessary imports vs DirectQuery, missing relationships, and lack of aggregations. Use Performance Analyzer to diagnose.

input: How do I fix a circular dependency error in DAX?
actual_output: Circular dependency errors occur when two or more calculated columns or tables reference each other. To fix: identify the cycle using the error message, break it by converting one calculated column to a measure (measures are lazily evaluated and don't create dependencies), or restructure your calculations to avoid mutual references.
expected_output: Circular dependencies happen when calculated columns reference each other. Fix by converting one to a measure (measures are lazily evaluated) or restructuring calculations to remove the cycle.
